title: Willow round base
subtitle: Setting up and weaving the slath base for a round willow basket
excerpt: The round base is the foundation of most willow baskets: a cross of thick sticks bound at the centre and opened out, then paired off with finer weavers until the base is the right diameter. Getting the base flat and even determines whether the finished basket sits steadily. This technique covers slath construction, opening, pairing, and finishing the base border.
sourceNotes: Traditional British stake-and-strand basketwork; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for round-base construction method and slath proportions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The cross of thick sticks at the centre of a round basket base; the structural foundation from which the base weaving radiates.
A two-rod weaving technique: two rods are laid together at the same point, then alternate around each stake; one goes over a stake while the other goes under; crossing each other between each stake.
The stiff, thicker rod that forms the structural upright of a basket; the weaving rods pass around the stakes to build the walls.

content.text: A round willow base starts with the
slath

: a cross of four thick base sticks, two pierced through two to hold the cross shape. The slath is opened out; the eight sticks separated and spread evenly; and then woven off with finer rods using the pairing technique. Most willow baskets of 30 cm diameter or larger use this round-base construction; the technique applies whether the finished basket is a log basket, a shopping basket, or a laundry hamper.
What you need
Base
sticks

: 8 rods of 8 to 10 mm diameter, cut to the base diameter plus 10 cm each side. For a 30 cm base, cut the sticks to 50 cm. Weavers: 16 fine rods of 4 to 5 mm diameter, at least 90 cm long. Buff willow (steamed) should be soaked for 30 minutes before use to restore flexibility; brown willow (unsteamed, dried) needs 1 to 2 hours. Green willow needs no soaking.
Step-by-step

Construct the slath: take 4 base sticks and use the bodkin to pierce through the centre of each, creating a slot. Thread the other 4 sticks through these slots to form a cross; 4 sticks in one direction piercing 4 sticks in the other. Slide the cross to the centre of all 8 sticks. The cross should feel firm; if it slides, the bodkin holes are too large; re-pierce with a smaller entry point.
Tie in: fold two fine weavers in half over one group of 4 sticks and work one round of
pairing
around the slath, treating each group of 4 sticks as a single unit. Work one complete round to anchor the weavers before opening.

Open the slath: on the second pairing round, separate the 4-stick groups into pairs, then into singles on the third round. By the end of three rounds, each of the 8 sticks is an individual stake radiating from the centre. Space them evenly; 45 degrees apart.

Continue pairing around the 8 individual stakes until the base reaches the intended diameter. Join in new weavers by laying the new rod alongside the old one, overlapping by 3 stake-gaps, and continuing with the new rod. The join should fall on the underside of the base.

Finish the base: trim the ends of the weavers close on the underside with the sloyd knife. Trim the base stick ends to 2 cm beyond the last weaving row. Press the base flat on a table; a correctly woven base lies flat without weight holding it down. Any crown (doming) in the base indicates uneven tension in the pairing rows.
What a correct base looks like

The finished base is flat, the 8 stakes radiate evenly, the weaving rows are at consistent tension; neither too tight (which pulls the base into a dome) nor too loose (which makes the weave open and floppy). Turn the base over: the underside should show clean, even crossing points at every stake, not a tangle of overlapping joins. The top surface should be smooth enough to slide on a table without rocking.
Common mistakes
Opening the slath too early (before two rounds of pairing are complete): the slath has no anchor and the sticks spring open unevenly. Complete two full rounds before opening.

Uneven stake spacing after opening: the weaving cannot correct for stakes that are not evenly distributed. Use a pencil mark on the weavers to check spacing before each round, or count strokes per stake to keep tension consistent.
Joins on the top face of the base: joins show as a slight thickening in the weave. Place all joins on the underside where they will not be visible in the finished basket.
